aliases: Oat bar with nuts and dried fruit

english: Oat bar with nuts and dried fruit

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for muesli bar; not required for this food category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard muesli bar weighing approximately 35 g, typical of commercially available single-serve bars.

nutrient_source.title: Muesli bar (nuts or fruits) — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for 'Muesli bar (nuts or fruits)' with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for muesli/granola bars (USDA SR Legacy #19016) as approximately 2.5 g/100 g.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 61 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for muesli bar (oat-based, with dried fruit and nuts), which reports a GI range of 54–65 for commercially produced muesli bars; midpoint 61 used. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 50.8 × 0.35) / 100 = 10.8, rounded to 11.2 using 35 g serving: (61 × 18.28) / 100 ≈ 11.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (18 g/100 g) with moderate sodium (84 mg/100 g); excess dietary sugar promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation, raising blood pressure.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit muesli bar intake to half a bar (≈17–20 g) per occasion and select varieties with no added honey or glucose syrup to keep sugar below 5 g per serving, per AHA 2018 dietary sugar guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 18 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g threshold for diabetes-suitable foods; medium GI of 61 combined with concentrated sugars from dried fruits, honey, and glucose syrup poses significant postprandial glycaemic risk.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption of muesli bars with added sugars; if consumed, restrict to a 20–25 g portion paired with a protein source such as plain Greek yogurt to attenuate the glucose response, per ADA 2023 Standards of Care.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.5 g/100 g) and high sugar (18 g/100 g) contribute to dyslipidaemia risk; however, oats provide beta-glucan fibre (5.8 g/100 g total fibre) and nuts contribute unsaturated fats with cardioprotective potential.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, choose muesli bars with ≥3 g beta-glucan oat fibre, no added glucose syrup, and saturated fat below 2 g per serving, consistent with AHA heart-healthy dietary patterns; limit to one small bar (30–35 g) per day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 18 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g RA refined-sugar threshold, promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine production; the nut component provides some anti-inflammatory vitamin E but does not offset the sugar-driven inflammatory burden.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid muesli bars with added honey, glucose syrup, or dried fruits; plain unsalted nuts are a preferable anti-inflammatory snack alternative, per EULAR anti-inflammatory dietary principles.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A muesli bar containing nuts or fruits is a commercially produced, compressed snack bar made from rolled oats bound together with sugars such as honey and glucose syrup, combined with nuts (commonly almonds, cashews, or peanuts) and dried fruits (such as raisins, cranberries, or apricots), and vegetable oil. The bar is typically baked or cold-pressed into a rectangular shape and wrapped for single-serve convenience. Texture ranges from chewy to crunchy depending on the binding agent and baking method. Flavour is sweet with nutty and fruity notes. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 373 kcal, 5.6 g protein, 15.3 g fat, 50.8 g carbohydrate, 18 g sugar, 5.8 g dietary fibre, 84 mg sodium, 29 mg calcium, and 1.6 mg iron. The high sugar content (18 g/100 g) makes it unsuitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension without strict portion control. The oat and nut base provides meaningful dietary fibre and unsaturated fats. Commonly consumed as a breakfast on-the-go or mid-morning snack. Glycemic index is approximately 61 (medium), making portion size critical for glycaemic management.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a muesli bar?
Is a muesli bar good for weight loss?
What is the sugar content of a muesli bar?
Is a granola bar the same as a muesli bar?
Can I eat a muesli bar if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of a muesli bar?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the canonical input record. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest match and documented. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for oat-based muesli bars.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB Foodo for this entry): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food and are not available in USDA SR Legacy at the level of specificity required for this generic formulation. Saturated fat (2.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #19016 (granola bars, hard, plain) as the closest approved-source analogue; this is an adaptation and is documented accordingly. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. The food is classified as Western/International and local_names fields are appropriately null. All core macros (protein_g 5.6, fat_g 15.3, carbohydrate_g 50.8) are non-zero and require no zero-macro justification.
